readerLens: THE BY-THE-NUMBERS READER — I weight the academic/quant gate, the test profile, and whether a no-score waiver file actually clears the bar against a record-737, 3.64-GPA, 24%-admit class. I am precise about where 3.54/no-score/no-Calculus sits in the 80% bands and whether the waiver would even be granted before asking whether it helps. My verdict: the stats are a CLEARED gate here, not a liability — a 3.54 Economics degree with A's in intermediate micro/macro and a self-coded pricing engine is a legitimately quant-strong waiver case (markedly stronger than the rubric's 3.45 Poli-Sci worked example), so the no-score does not drag this file the way it drags a typical quant-light vet. The drag on this file is NOT the numbers; it is a near-empty 'Why Stern' and a goals essay built for a deleted prompt. Numerically strong-on-target; the risk is execution on positioning, not the gate.

dimensionScores.note: 3.54 sits BELOW the 3.64 class average and only mid-80%-band (3.36-3.89) — not a 5 on GPA alone. But this is an ECONOMICS degree (Cum Laude, Dept Honors, Dean's List x6) with A's in Intermediate Micro, Intermediate Macro, and Macro Policy, plus B+ in Intro Statistics AND Fundamentals of Programming — exactly the 'analytical/quant degree with strong grades' the band-5 descriptor names. The single blemish keeping this off a 5: the transcript tops out at Pre-Calculus, NO Calculus. For a by-the-numbers reader that is the one real soft spot, because it is the obvious question a Stern reader asks of any waiver econ major. Net: solidly quant-readable, well above the rubric's 3.45 Poli-Sci example, materially below a 3.89/STEM 5. A strong 4.

No GMAT/GRE/EA; pursuing the by-request FT waiver (NOT the part-time auto-vet waiver, which does not exist for FT — he has this right in the file). Per the band, a GRANTED, well-justified waiver from a quant-STRONG profile scores 3, not 1 — and this profile qualifies as quant-strong (econ A's, applied modeling, self-coded venture), so it is NOT the band-1 'quant-light/overrepresented waiver that cannot demonstrate readiness.' Caveat I hold as a hard-nosed reader: the waiver is not yet secured (request targeted ~Aug 4), and mbaMission's own caution is that a thin-quant record may not even be GRANTED — his is not thin, so grant is probable, but a 198-word waiver statement that leans on Pre-Calc-capped coursework is doing real work. Scores 3; would be a clean 3 once granted.

This is the engine of the file and it is unambiguously band-5. Quantified, civilian-legible, escalating scope: $13.1M/903-asset drawdown across 7 forward bases tracked to CENTCOM; an 81% cycle-time cut (21->4 days) brokered across multiple parties; $592K cache recovered; $6.1M/5-country deployment enabling 108 ops; a $250K/2,000-item account reconciled to zero. Real-time dashboards spanning 21 countries adopted as the command's daily decision tool. Promotion to Captain with OIC scope. The resume is already civilian-translated and quantified per the dossier. This is precisely the 'reduced downtime 40%, saved $X' rewrite that S2S says vets fail to produce — he produces it. No weakness here.

Short-term goal is specific, named, and credible: S&O consulting at Deloitte S&O / Kearney / EY-Parthenon, with a genuinely smart 'build-up + wind-down = full lifecycle' framing that ties the goal to his actual Pacific (stand-up) and Middle East (drawdown) record — that linkage is band-5 reasoning. Held to a 4, not 5, for two by-the-numbers reasons: (1) no stated Plan B / contingency anywhere, and the Clear Admit interview reports show Stern reliably asks for one; (2) the long-term is explicitly unresolved in the dossier (consulting vs. scaling Silverlinings), which a sharp reader will read as the goal not being fully owned. Also note: logistics-officer-to-consulting is the 'paved road' S2S flags (80% of vets start there, 30% end there) — the specificity rescues it from band-1, but the default-consulting gravity is real.

dimensionScores.dimension: Stern-Specific Fit & Why Stern / Why Now
dimensionScores.note: THE WEAKEST DIMENSION and the file's true ceiling. The current materials contain essentially ZERO non-portable Why-Stern: no named course, no professor, no club, no experiential offering (no Change:Studio, no Stern Consulting Corps, no MVC specifics), no alumni/event contact. The deleted 'Change' essay gestures at 'a veterans cohort that has walked this exact road' and 'the school that will ease this transition more than any other' — generic and pasteable. The 150-word answer offers 'client reps through its consulting community' and 'two years inside the city where these firms live' — that is portable to Columbia (his actual #2) verbatim. The live 500-word prompt's load-bearing word is 'NECESSARY'; nothing in the current file argues Stern is necessary vs. any NYC top program. This is the single most-cited career-essay failure mode in the knowledge base. Band-2 because there ARE real raw hooks (NYC consulting ecosystem, Fertitta, veteran transition) to build on — but as written, it is near band-1.

Genuinely strong and differentiated EQ material, which matters disproportionately at IQ+EQ-doctrine Stern. The 'leader decides what kind of day his people have / chose kindness' model from Col Johnson; the welcome-aboard onboarding package (institutionalizing tacit knowledge, pay-it-forward); mentoring junior Marines outside his chain; the Gen Hadir KLE where he 'restated the General's objection back in his own words until he felt understood' to land the 81% cut — that is a vivid, specific empathy-under-pressure anecdote, exactly Stern's four EQ pillars in action. Self-awareness is present via the lacrosse-captaincy-I-was-afraid-to-want beat. Held to 4 not 5 because that self-aware weakness is pre-Marine and somewhat packaged; the file is rich on empathy/self-management, slightly lighter on a current, candidly-owned professional weakness. Still well above median.

Clear band-5. Formal command leadership (OIC, 8-person section, $1M budget) plus mission-scale leadership across coalition environments; integrity/maturity signals throughout (Secret clearance, three joint awards); and a genuine community/mentorship dimension beyond the job — mentoring Marines outside his chain, the 313-lb food-pantry conditioning hike, the welcome-aboard package. Character is the 'C people underweight' and he is strong on it. No concerns.

Logistics/operations officer is a CROWDED veteran sub-pool and consulting is its default exit — this is exactly the saturated-bucket trap. What pulls him to a 4: (1) a solo-coded founder venture (Silverlinings) built while serving and on deployment — rare and concrete, not a hobby; (2) quantified coalition-scale breadth (21-country dashboard, 7-base drawdown) beyond the typical logistics file; (3) an unusually warm, specific EQ narrative. Per the band, a common background made distinctive via quantified results + a credible non-default thread + dimensions beyond work = 4. Not a 5 because the stated post-MBA goal IS the default (consulting), so the differentiation lives in the backstory rather than the destination — a top-decile file would point the distinctiveness at a non-default goal too.

The writing is genuinely good — clear, low-jargon, civilian-legible, voice-consistent, and the 'sees inefficiency -> builds the fix himself' throughline is a real consulting thesis. The Pick Six is a standout: six DISTINCT, personal-leading dimensions (welding, self-coded venture, Ashley, lacrosse-fear, food-pantry ruck, dinner table) with why-it-matters captions and an explicit 'none of them happened in a uniform' frame — that is a coherent themed portrait, not a second resume, and the captions carry the why. That alone supports a 4. Capped at 4 (not 5) by a process reality: the strongest prose currently lives in a DELETED prompt (the 350-word Change essay), so the craft is proven but not yet deployed against the live 500-word prompt; images still TBD; Q3 of the endorsement still owed.

The substance is potentially band-5 — a vivid, specific EQ anecdote (the Gen Hadir KLE, 'an emotional innovation not a logistics one'), a credible endorser who signed both end-of-tour citations and worked beside him daily for five months. But two concrete deductions keep it at 3: (1) Col Johnson is a FORMER, not current, supervisor — the knowledge base flags a missing current-supervisor endorsement as 'a meaningful negative,' mitigated here only by the optional-essay explanation (he hasn't disclosed his intent to leave to his current command — a legitimate, well-stated reason, but still a deviation the reader must accept); (2) Q3 (the most-important-feedback story) is still owed and is, per the file itself, the 'last credibility blocker' — an incomplete endorsement cannot score above median. Solid-but-not-yet-landed; 3.

R1 timing, full eligibility, U.S. citizen veteran, clean process discipline, organized timeline (waiver request ~3 weeks pre-app, endorsement packet sequenced). Veteran is a recognized-but-modest tailwind. The only thing keeping it off a 5 is the latent process risk that the file was built for the wrong (deleted) essay set — if not repositioned, that becomes a real process/non-compliance issue. Most of the round and veteran magnitude is captured in Section-D modifiers to avoid double-count, so this stays a small 4.

asWrittenRisk: If he submits the CURRENT materials without repositioning to the live prompts, the realistic odds drop roughly one full band — from ~50-65% to ~33-42% (low end of Strong collapsing into On-target/Reach). Mechanism: the 500-word Professional Aspirations essay would be answered by either a 150-word short answer or a 350-word essay written for DELETED prompts — effectively non-responsive, reading as 'didn't read the instructions' (a named ding driver), and the already-weak portable Why-Stern (dim 5 = 2/5) goes fully unaddressed against a prompt that demands Stern be shown 'necessary.' Quantitatively, treat it as a process non-compliance hit (-3 to -6) plus the lost opportunity to lift dim 5, pulling the adjusted total from ~82 down into the high-60s/low-70s. The underlying profile (the band-5 professional record, the quant-strong waiver case, the strong Pick Six) is good enough that he is not eliminated — but he would be competing at On-target (~33-42%) instead of Strong, having left 15+ points of admit probability on the table for a 100%-fixable reason. The gap between as-written and well-executed is the single highest-leverage fact about this application.
scholarshipFlag: Admit-strong; general merit-aid UNLIKELY; Fertitta scholarship PLAUSIBLE-but-not-automatic. Per the corpus-mandated separate test: scholarship-competitive requires (score >= class avg) AND (R1/R2) AND (adjusted >=72). He meets R1 and the >=72 threshold (81.8) but FAILS the score requirement — a granted waiver/no-score removes the strongest merit-aid lever, so open merit aid is unlikely even though it is admit-neutral. However, as a credible, differentiated veteran he routes to the separate Fertitta track ($30k flat tuition, merit-based, NOT all admitted vets receive it, no separate application). Net: funding, if it comes, most likely arrives via Fertitta rather than open merit aid — and Fertitta is plausible-but-not-guaranteed given the strong-but-not-top-decile file. He should also note the $20k/yr living stipend (Ch.33/Yellow Ribbon, mutually exclusive with merit scholarship) and the application fee waiver as separate veteran levers.
